1: haʾăzînû ʿammî tôrātî — listen, O my people, to my instruction. Haʾăzîn (hiphil imperative of ʾāzan: to give ear, to incline the ear — a posture of attentive reception) opens this long historical poem with the register of wisdom instruction. Tôrāh here is not the Mosaic law specifically but instruction, teaching — the same word used for a teacher's guidance (Prov 1:8; 3:1). The poem functions as a wisdom meditation on history, not a law code.
2: ʾeptĕḥāh bĕmāshāl pî — I will open my mouth with a parable. Māshāl (parable, wisdom saying, comparison) + ḥîdôt (riddles, dark sayings, things requiring interpretation). Matthew 13:35 cites this verse as fulfilled in Jesus's parabolic teaching: the long-hidden mysteries of Psalm 78 are the very things Jesus opens in parable form. Ḥîdāh (riddle, enigma) — a truth that requires effort to decode, not merely information to receive.
3: ʾăsher shāmaʿnû wĕnēdāʿēm — what we have heard and come to know. The oral tradition — shāmaʿ (to hear, the primary mode of Torah reception in a pre-literate culture) and yādaʿ (to know by experience, to understand through relationship). waʾăbôtênû sipperû-lānû — what our ancestors passed down to us. The chain of transmission is already two generations deep before the current speaker — hearing the history of YHWH is itself a historical act.
4: lōʾ nĕkhahēd mibĕnêhem — we will not conceal from their children. Kāḥad (to conceal, hide) — the deliberate decision against suppression. lĕdôr ʾaḥărôn mĕsappĕrîm tĕhillôt YHWH — we will tell the next generation of the LORD's praiseworthy deeds. Tĕhillôt (praises, acts worthy of praise) — the history of YHWH's acts is itself a form of his praise. Telling history truthfully is an act of worship.
5: wayyāqem ʿēdût bĕyaʿăqōb wĕtôrāh śām bĕyisrāʾêl — he established a formal testimony in Jacob and ordained a law in Israel. ʿĒdût (testimony, decree — the formal covenant stipulation, cf. Ps 19:7; 25:10) — the divine establishment of the transmission requirement. The parents' obligation to teach is not cultural preference but divinely ordained covenant structure. ʾăsher ṣiwwāh ʾet-ʾăbôtênû lĕhôdîʿām lĕbĕnêhem — which he commanded our ancestors to teach their children.
6: lĕmaʿan yēdĕʿû dôr ʾaḥărôn bānîm yiwwāledû — so that the generation to come would know — even children not yet born. The purpose clause extends the transmission chain forward into theoretical infinity: children not yet conceived are already the intended audience of the teaching. The phrase bānîm yiwwāledû (children who will be born) — future generations already within the scope of the divine teaching mandate.
7: wĕyāśîmû bĕʾĕlōhîm kislām — so they would place their confident hope in God. Kesel (confidence, hope — from a root meaning thickness, fatness; the solid, well-founded confidence of one who is not naively hopeful but substantially grounded) — not mere optimism but trust founded on historical evidence. wĕlōʾ yishkĕḥû maʿallĕlê ʾēl — and would not forget what God has done. The entire transmission chain serves the goal of non-forgetting — what Psalm 78 will show is precisely how catastrophic forgetting is.
8: wĕlōʾ yihyû kĕʾăbôtām dôr sôrēr ûmôreh — and would not be like their ancestors — a stubborn and rebellious generation. Sôrēr ûmôreh (stubborn and rebellious) is the technical phrase of Deut 21:18-21 for the incorrigible son who faces execution — applied here to a whole generation. dôr lōʾ-hēkhîn libbô wĕlōʾ-neʾĕmānāh ʾet-ʾēl rûḥô — a generation that did not fix its heart on God and whose spirit was not faithful to him. Unfaithfulness is first a matter of the heart's orientation, then behavior follows.
9: bĕnê ʾeprāyim nôshĕqê rômê-qāshet — the men of Ephraim, though armed with bows. The opening of the Ephraim indictment — Ephraim (the dominant northern tribe) is the paradigm of failure throughout this psalm, setting up the contrast with Judah's election (v68). hāpĕkhû bĕyôm qĕrāb — turned back when the day of battle came. The hiphil of hāphak (to turn, reverse) — armed warriors who reversed course in battle. The specific event is debated (the conquest? a particular battle?) but the theological meaning is clear: military equipment without covenant faithfulness produces failure.
10: lōʾ shāmĕrû berît ʾĕlōhîm — they failed to keep God's covenant. The covenant (berît) as the governing framework — Ephraim's military failure in v9 is explained theologically here. ûbĕtôrātô māʾănû lālākheth — and refused to live by his instruction. Māʾan (to refuse — a deliberate, volitional rejection, not mere neglect). The law as the locus of covenant faithfulness: refusing the Torah is refusing the covenant.
11: wayyishkĕḥû ʿălîlôtāyw — they forgot what he had done. Shākhah (to forget) — the central sin of this psalm. The verb appears repeatedly (vv11, 42; and the command not to forget in vv7, 35) — forgetting YHWH's acts is not innocent lapse but covenant unfaithfulness. wĕniplĕʾôtāyw ʾăsher herʾām — and the wonders he had shown them. The wonders (niplĕʾôt) were shown (herʾām, hiphil: caused them to see) — the audience was present, the deeds were public, the forgetting is inexcusable.
12: negĕd ʾăbôtām ʿāśāh pheleʾ — he performed remarkable deeds in plain sight of their ancestors. Negĕd (before, in front of, openly) — the miracles were not private or hidden but performed publicly. bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim śĕdēh-ṣōʿan — in the land of Egypt, in the region of Zoan. Ṣōʿan (Zoan, the Egyptian city near where Israel lived during the bondage — Tanis/Avaris in the Nile Delta) is the historical anchor: these were real events at a real location.
13: bāqaʿ yām wayyaʿăbîrēm — he split the sea and brought them through. Bāqaʿ (to split, cleave — the same verb for the splitting of the rock in v15) — the primary act of the Exodus. wayyaṣṣēb-māyim kĕmô-nēd — he made the water stand in walls. Nēd (heap, mound — used of the wall of water in Exod 15:8; Josh 3:13) — the water piled up in a solid heap, defying its own nature at God's command.
14: wayyanhēm beʿānān yômām — in the daytime he led them with a cloud. Nāhāh (to lead, guide) — the divine leading through both day and night elements. wĕkhol-hallaylāh bĕʾôr ʾesh — and all night long with a column of fire. The cloud (protection from the Egyptian sun) and fire (light and warmth at night) are the twin provisions of the wilderness: the natural needs of a desert journey met by supernatural means.
15: yĕbaqquaʿ ṣurîm bammidbār — he split open rocks in the wilderness. The same verb bāqaʿ (to split, v13) now applied to rocks — the God who split the sea also splits rock. wayyasht kĕtĕhōmôt rabbāh — and gave them water to drink as from the great deep. Tĕhôm rabbāh (the great deep — the primordial subterranean ocean of Gen 1:2; 7:11) — the water from the rock is not merely a miraculous spring but draws on the same cosmic deep that YHWH controls at creation.
16: wayyôṣiʾ nōzĕlîm miṣṣûr — he brought streams out of the rock. Nōzĕlîm (flowing streams — from nāzal: to flow, trickle) — not a single gush but sustained flowing water. wayyôred kĕnahărôt māyim — and made the water flow down in rivers. Nāhār (river — the word for a substantial, navigable waterway) — what came from desert rock was not a trickle but a river. The extravagance of divine provision is the point.
17: wayyôsîpû ʿôd laḥăṭōʾ-lô — yet they kept on sinning against him. The waw-consecutive with yāsaph (to add, to continue) — they added sin upon sin, not a single lapse but a continuing pattern. lamrôt ʿelyôn baṣṣiyyāh — provoking the Most High in the wilderness. Mārah (to be rebellious, to provoke by opposition) + ʿelyôn (Most High) — the specific name emphasizes the audacity: provoking the highest authority in the driest place, surrounded by the evidence of miraculous provision.
18: waynassû ʾēl bĕlibbām — they deliberately tested God in their hearts. Nāsāh (to test, to put to the proof — the same verb as in the Temptation narratives, Matt 4:7 citing Deut 6:16) — testing God is not innocent inquiry but insolent demanding. lishʾōl-ʾōkhel lĕnapshām — demanding food to satisfy their cravings. Nepeš (soul, appetite) — the demand is for physical satisfaction. The test is whether God's provision was sufficient or whether they could impose their own agenda on it.
19: waydabbĕrû bĕʾĕlōhîm — they spoke against God. The preposition bĕ (in, against) with the speaking verb indicates hostile speech — speaking at or against God, not to him. ʾāmĕrû hayûkhal ʾēl laʿărōkh shulḥān bammidbār — they said, 'Can God really lay out a meal here in the wilderness?' Shulḥān (table — the piece of furniture for formal eating, not just food) — the question demands not mere sustenance but a proper table, a served meal. The irony is that God had already provided the cloud, fire, and water; the question treats all of it as insufficient.
20: hēn hikkāh-ṣûr wayyāzûbû māyim — look, he hit a rock and water poured out. The accusative hēn (behold, look) — the concession before the challenge. They acknowledge the water miracle: wayyāzûbû māyim (and water flowed abundantly). hagam-leḥem yûkhal tēt ʾim-yākhîn sheʾēr lĕʿammô — but can he also supply bread? Can he provide meat for his whole people? The challenge escalates from the miraculous already accomplished to a demand for more. The unanswered question is immediately answered by God's action in vv23-28.
21: lākhēn shāmaʿ YHWH wayyitʿabbār — therefore the LORD heard what they said and became furious. ʿAbar (hithpael: to be provoked to fury — used of floodwaters overflowing; divine anger as an overflowing) — God heard the hostile speech of v19 and responded with commensurate anger. wĕʾesh niśśĕqāh bĕyaʿăqōb — a fire broke out against Jacob. The fire of divine judgment (Num 11:1-3) — literal fire at the borders of the camp when the same complaint arose.
22: kî lōʾ heʾĕmînû bĕʾĕlōhîm — because they refused to believe in God. The hiphil of ʾāman (to trust, to place firm confidence in) — belief is trust-commitment, not intellectual assent. The diagnosis of the sin: the demand for bread and meat was a symptom of fundamental distrust. wĕlōʾ bāṭĕḥû bîshûʿātô — and put no trust in his power to save. Yĕshûʿāh (salvation, the capacity to rescue) — they had seen the Exodus and doubted God's willingness/ability to save them in the wilderness.
23: wayyĕṣaw shĕḥāqîm mimmaʿal — yet he had already commanded the clouds above. The command-verb ṣiwwāh (to command) — even while angry (v21), God had already issued the provision order. The divine provision is not earned by Israel's subsequent faith; it precedes their complaint. wĕdaltê shāmayim pātāḥ — and opened the doors of heaven. Daltê shāmayim (the doors/gates of heaven) — the heavenly storehouses opened, the same image as in Gen 7:11 for the floodgates and Mal 3:10 for the windows of heaven.
24: wayyamṭēr ʿălêhem mān leʾĕkhōl — he had rained down manna for them to eat. The verb māṭar (to rain) applied to the manna — provision from the sky, like rain, but unlike rain. wĕdĕgan shāmayim nātan lāmô — and had given them grain from heaven. Dĕgan shāmayim (grain of heaven) — heavenly bread contrasted with earthly grain. John 6:31 quotes this phrase in the Bread of Life discourse: Jesus is the true bread from heaven, of which the manna was a type.
25: leḥem ʾabbîrîm ʾākhal ʾîsh — human beings ate the food of angels. ʾAbbîrîm (the mighty ones — the term used for powerful warriors and, by extension in some traditions, for heavenly beings/angels, cf. LXX's bread of angels in the Septuagint rendering here). The manna is described as food of the supernatural realm. ṣēdāh shālah lāhem lāśābăʿ — he sent them abundant provisions. Śābēaʿ (abundance, fullness — satisfaction beyond adequacy) — not starvation-rations but lavish supply.
26: yassēʿ qādîm bashshāmāyim — he caused an east wind to blow through the sky. The east and south winds are the instruments of the quail-provision (cf. Num 11:31 where the wind brings quail from the sea). Nāsaʿ (hiphil: to cause to set out, to make journey) — wind as a divine agent dispatched on a mission. waynahēg bĕʿuzzô tēmān — by his power he drove the south wind. God driving the wind as a charioteer drives horses — creation obeys its Creator's commands.
27: wayyamṭēr ʿălêhem kĕʿāphār shĕʾēr — he rained meat on them as thick as dust. ʿĀphār (dust — the comparison is to the innumerable grains of dust, not to anything negative). ûkĕḥôl yammîm ʿôph kānāph — and birds with wings as numerous as grains of sea sand. The comparison to sand of the sea (the Abrahamic promise vocabulary, Gen 22:17) is ironic: the quantity promised to Abraham's descendants is here applied to the birds sent to feed them.
28: wayyappēl bĕqereb maḥănēhû — he dropped it right in the middle of their camp. Nāpal (hiphil: to cause to fall, to drop) — the quail not driven to the edges requiring effort to gather but dropped in the center of the camp, immediately accessible. sābîb lĕmishkĕnōtāyw — all around where they lived. The provision surrounds their dwellings — an image of enveloping, excessive generosity.
29: wayyōʾkĕlû wayyisbĕʾû meʾōd — they ate and were completely satisfied. Śābaʿ meʾōd (were completely satisfied — satisfied to excess, absolutely full) — God gave exactly what the craving demanded and more. wĕtaʾăwātām yābîʾ lāhem — he had given them exactly what they wanted. Taʾăwāh (craving, desire — the same word used of the Tree of the Knowledge in Gen 3:6) — their craving was satisfied. But the satisfaction of craving does not produce faithfulness; it sets up the catastrophe of v30-31.
30: lōʾ-zārû mitaʾăwātām — they had not turned from their craving. The verb zûr (to be a stranger to, to be estranged from) — they had not become estranged from their craving; the desire persisted even while being satisfied. ʿôd ʾokhlām bĕphîhem — the food was still in their mouths. The chilling timing — the meat still between their teeth when judgment fell (cf. Num 11:33 literally). Satiation did not produce gratitude; it produced no movement of the heart at all.
31: wĕʾap ʾĕlōhîm ʿālāh bāhem — when God's wrath came down on them. ʾAph (nostril, anger — the breathing-wrath of God) rising against them. wayyahărōg bĕmishmannêhem — and slew the strongest among them. Mishman (the fat ones, the choice ones — the best of the people, those in prime physical condition). ûbĕḥûrê yisrāʾêl hikhriʿ — and struck down the young men of Israel. Kāraʿ (to cause to kneel, to bring down) — the strong are specifically targeted: divine judgment falls on capacity, not just weakness.
32: bĕkhol-zōʾt ḥāṭĕʾû ʿôd — despite all of this, they went on sinning. The summarizing bĕkhol-zōʾt (despite all of this — through all the plagues, the provision, the judgment) — the pattern continues. wĕlōʾ heʾĕmînû bĕniplĕʾôtāyw — and refused to believe even his miracles. The miracles were not hidden but public; disbelief is not ignorance but willful refusal. Faith should have been the natural response to what they witnessed; its absence is moral failure.
33: waykhal bĕhebel yĕmêhem — so he made their days dissolve into emptiness. Hebel (breath, vapor, emptiness — Qoheleth's signature word) — the wasted potential of their lives reduced to vapor. ûshĕnôtām babbĕhālāh — and their years end in sudden fright. Bĕhālāh (terror, sudden dread — from bāhal: to terrify, to agitate) — not peaceful old age but years that end in shock. The wilderness generation died without reaching the goal; their years were emptied of purpose by their own unfaithfulness.
34: ʾim-hăragām ûdĕrāshûhû — whenever he struck them down, they would search for him. The conditional ʾim marking a repeated pattern: each strike produces a temporary seeking. Dārash (to seek, to inquire — the word of serious, purposeful inquiry) — but the sequel shows it is crisis-driven, not genuine. wĕshābû wĕshiḥărû ʾēl — they would turn around and sincerely seek God. Shāḥar (to seek eagerly in the morning, to seek earnestly — from shaḥar: dawn) — the urgency of early-morning seeking. But the sincerity is apparent, not real, as v35-37 show.
35: wayyizkĕrû kî ʾĕlōhîm ṣûrām — they recalled that God was their rock. Ṣûr (rock) — the crisis-memory of God's attribute as fortress and foundation. wĕʾēl ʿelyôn gōʾălām — and the Most High God was their redeemer. Gāʾal (kinsman-redeemer) — the memory of the Exodus redemption returns under pressure. The titles Rock and Redeemer are exactly what Psalm 77 closed with; the memory is accurate. But memory and transformation are not the same thing.
36: waypattûhû bĕphîhem — but they only said what he wanted to hear. Pittāh (piel of pāthāh: to entice, to flatter, to deceive with words — the same word used of the seductive enticement in Prov 1:10; 7:21) — they flattered God with their confession-speech. ûbilshônām yĕkazzĕbû-lô — they spoke to him with lying tongues. Kāzab (to lie, to be deceitful) — the repentance-speech was deceptive. Crisis produces the words of repentance without the reality of it.
37: wĕlibbām lōʾ-khên ʿimmô — their hearts were not truly directed toward him. Kēn (right, fixed, directed — the heart in the right position, oriented toward God) — a diagnosis: the heart-orientation never changed. wĕlōʾ neʾĕmĕnû bĕbĕrîtô — they were not faithful to his covenant. ʾĀman (niphal: to be established, faithful, reliable) — the covenant requires consistent faithfulness, not crisis-driven performance. External words without internal transformation do not satisfy the covenant.
38: wĕhûʾ raḥûm yĕkhappēr ʿāwōn — yet he, being full of compassion, covered over their guilt. The theological pivot of the entire psalm: wĕhûʾ (but he — the emphatic pronoun marking the divine counter to human failure). Raḥûm (compassionate — from reḥem: womb; the deep, inward, maternal compassion). Yĕkhappēr (he covers/atones — the piel of kāpar, the technical term for atonement) — guilt covered, not merely overlooked. wĕlōʾ-yashḥît wĕhirbāh lĕhāshîb ʾappô — and did not destroy them; again and again he turned back his anger. The repeated restraint — not once but habitually, structurally. Divine compassion is not a single exception but a pattern that constitutes God's character.
39: wayyizkōr kî-bāśār hēmmāh — he remembered that they were only flesh. Bāśār (flesh — the creaturely, mortal material of human existence) — God's memory of the creature's constitution is the theological basis for the compassion of v38. This is not low expectations but covenant grace: the Creator factors in the creature's weakness. rûaḥ hōlēkh wĕlōʾ yāshûb — a passing wind that does not come back. Rûaḥ (spirit, wind, breath) that passes and does not return — the full Qoheleth meditation on human transience (cf. Eccl 1:4-6) compressed into one clause. God's anger is restrained by his awareness of human mortality.
40: kammāh yamrûhû bammidbār — how many times they defied him in the wilderness. Kammāh (how many — an exclamation of enumerable but uncounted frequency). Mārah (to rebel, to be bitter — the same root as Miriam/Marah, the bitter water of Exod 15:23) — the wilderness rebellions are numerous enough to resist counting. yaʿaṣîbûhû bishshĕmāmāh — and caused him grief in the wasteland. ʿĀṣab (to hurt, to cause grief, to wound with sorrow — used of human grief and of divine grief: Gen 6:6) — the Psalmist does not hesitate to ascribe genuine emotional pain to God at Israel's rebellion.
41: wayyāshûbû waynassû ʾēl — they kept turning back and testing God. The waw-consecutive marks the recurring cycle: return → test again. wĕqĕdôsh yisrāʾêl hitʾimmârû — pressing against the Holy One of Israel. Hitʾammēr (hithpael of ʾāmar: to press against, to provoke by insolent speech — a rare form) — pushing against the limits of the Holy One. Qĕdôsh yisrāʾêl (Holy One of Israel) is Isaiah's characteristic divine epithet — its use here may reflect the Isaianic tradition or share its origin in Asaphite worship.
42: lōʾ zākhĕrû ʾet-yādô — they forgot what his hand had done. Zākar (to remember — here negated: not to remember) — the hand of God, the instrument of Exodus power. yôm ʾăsher-pādām miṣṣār — and the day he rescued them from their oppressor. Pādāh (to redeem/rescue by payment or force) — the redemption day of the Exodus. The forgetting of the Exodus is the root cause of the rebellion in Canaan; if the Exodus is remembered, its implications for trust in the wilderness follow naturally.
43: ʾăsher-śām bĕmiṣrāyim ʾôtōtāyw — how he had displayed his miraculous signs in Egypt. ʾŌt (sign — the Exodus plagues as divine signs, cf. Exod 10:1-2: the signs were intended so that the story would be told to future generations). ûmôphĕtāyw biśdēh-ṣōʿan — and his wonders in the region of Zoan. Môphēt (wonder, portent — paired with ʾôt throughout Deuteronomy's plague summaries: Deut 6:22; 7:19; 26:8). The Zoan location anchors the wonders in history.
44: wayyahăpōkh lĕdām yĕʾōrêhem — he turned their rivers into blood. Hāphak (to turn, overturn, convert) — the first plague (Exod 7:17-21). Yĕʾōr (the Nile — the Egyptian term for the river, not the standard Hebrew nāhār) — Egypt's life-giving river turned lethal. wĕnōzĕlêhem bilti yishĕtāyûn — so that they were unable to drink from their streams. The inversion of the rock-water provision (v15-16): God can give water from rock or take water from rivers.
45: yĕshallēaḥ bāhem ʿārōb wayyōkhlēm — he sent swarms of flies to devour them. ʿĀrōb (swarms — possibly a mixture of insects, traditionally flies) — the fourth plague (Exod 8:20-32). ûṣĕphardēaʿ wattishshĕḥîtēm — and frogs that brought ruin upon them. Ṣĕphardēaʿ (frogs — the second plague, Exod 8:1-15). The order here is not strictly Exodus order — the Psalm rearranges for rhetorical rather than chronological effect.
46: wayyittēn lĕḥāsîl yĕbûlām — he handed their harvest over to the locust. Ḥāsîl (locust — the devouring stage of the locust, from ḥāsal: to consume, finish off). wîgîʿām lāʾarbeh — and their labor to the swarming caterpillar. ʾArbeh (the swarming locust — the eighth plague, Exod 10:1-20). The pairing of two locust-type insects emphasizes total agricultural devastation: whatever survived the first was finished by the second.
47: yaharōg babbārād gaphnam — he struck their vines down with hail. The seventh plague (Exod 9:13-35). Bārād (hail — the destructive ice storm that devastated Egyptian agriculture). wĕshiqmôtām bĕḥanāmāl — and their sycamore trees with killing frost. Shiqmāh (sycamore-fig tree — a resilient tree important to Egyptian agriculture and to the poor as a food source). Ḥanāmāl (killing frost, blight — rare word) — even the hardiest trees were destroyed.
48: wayyasgēr labbārād bĕʿîrām — he surrendered their cattle to the hail. Sāgar (to shut up, surrender to — used of handing prisoners to their captors; God hands the Egyptian livestock to the hail as an executioner). ûmiqnêhem lārĕshāphîm — and their flocks to lightning strikes. Rĕsheph (flame, lightning bolt, burning fever — a divine fire-messenger) — the livestock killed by the electrical component of the hailstorm.
49: yĕshallēaḥ bām ḥărôn ʾappô — he unleashed his fierce anger upon them. Ḥărôn ʾap (the burning of the nostrils — fierce, blazing anger) — the full force of divine anger. ʿebrāh wāzaʿam wĕṣārāh mishlaḥat malʾăkhê rāʿîm — his wrath, indignation, and distress — a company of destroying messengers. Four terms for divine anger (ḥărôn + ʿebrāh + zaʿam + ṣārāh) accumulate. Malʾăkhê rāʿîm (messengers/angels of evil) — the destroying angels as divine instruments, distinct from God himself but serving his punitive purpose.
50: yĕpallēs nātîb lĕʾappô — he made a clear way for his wrath. Pālas (piel: to weigh out, level, make a clear path) — God smoothing the road for his own anger to travel without obstacle. lōʾ-ḥāśak mimmāwet napshām — he did not withhold their lives from death. Ḥāśak (to withhold, spare) — no sparing, no restraint. wĕḥayyātām laddeber higîr — but surrendered them to the plague. Deber (plague, pestilence) — the fifth plague was specifically against livestock (Exod 9:1-7), but the cumulative effect is death throughout Egypt.
51: wayyak kol-bĕkhôr bĕmiṣrāyim — he killed all the firstborn in Egypt. The climactic tenth plague — the one that finally broke Pharaoh's resistance. rēʾshît ʾônîm bĕʾohŏlê ḥām — the prime of their strength in the dwellings of Ham. Rēʾshît ʾônîm (the firstfruits of their strength — the best offspring, the concentrated vigor of procreation). ʾOhŏlê ḥām (the tents/dwellings of Ham) — Egypt as the land of Ham, one of Noah's sons (Gen 10:6). The ethnic designation reminds: what falls is not just a political power but a family of humanity that opposed God's redemptive purpose.
52: wayyassēʿ kattsōʾn ʿammô — but he brought his own people out like sheep. The contrast with v51 is immediate: Egypt's prime is killed; Israel is led like a shepherd leads a flock. Nāsaʿ (to cause to journey, to lead) — the same root as the wilderness journeys of Numbers. waynah'gēm kammidbār kaʿēder — and led them through the wilderness like a flock. ʿĒder (flock, drove of livestock) — Israel is led through the wilderness as a whole flock, not scattered stragglers.
53: wayyanhēm lābeṭaḥ wĕlōʾ phāḥādû — he guided them in safety so that they had nothing to fear. Bāṭaḥ (securely, in safety — the state of being unafraid because adequately protected). Pāḥad (to be in dread, to be terrified) — negated: the divine leading produced freedom from fear. wĕʾet-ʾôyĕbêhem kissāh hayyām — the sea swallowed their enemies. The pursuing Egyptian army covered by the sea — the crossing secured not just Israel's exit but the permanent removal of the threat.
54: waybîʾēm ʾel-gĕbûl qodshô — he brought them to his sacred territory. Gĕbûl qodshô (the border/territory of his holiness — the land of Canaan as the sacred space where YHWH's presence would dwell). har-zeh qānĕtāh yĕmînô — to the mountain that his right hand had won. Qānāh (to acquire, create, possess — the same creation verb as Gen 14:19,22) — Zion as the mountain YHWH acquired through the conquest, not merely occupied. The right hand as the instrument of both Exodus power (Exod 15:6) and Conquest victory.
55: waygārash mippenêhem gôyim — he expelled the nations from before them. Gārash (to drive out, expel) — the Conquest as divine expulsion, not human military achievement. wayyappîlēm bĕḥebel naḥălāh — assigned their land as an inheritance by lot. Ḥebel (measuring line — the land surveyed by rope and distributed by lot; the same word used in Ps 16:6 for the pleasant lines of David's inheritance). wayyashken bĕʾohŏlêhem shibtê yisrāʾêl — and made the tribes of Israel to dwell in their territory. Settlement is the completion of the salvation arc that began with the Exodus.
56: waynassû wayyamrû ʾet-ʾĕlōhîm ʿelyôn — nevertheless they tested and defied the Most High God. The cycle resumes — Canaan does not break the pattern of wilderness rebellion. wĕʿēdôtāyw lōʾ shāmārû — and refused to keep his commandments. ʿĒdôt (testimonies, the covenant stipulations) — the same term as v5's establishment of the covenant structure. Having received the land, they refuse the law that governs it.
57: wayyissōgû wayyibgĕdû kaʾăbôtām — they turned back and acted faithlessly, just as their ancestors had. Sûg (to move back, to apostatize) — the movement is backward, away from God. nehepĕkhû kĕqeshet remiyyāh — they bent away like a defective bow. Qeshet remiyyāh (a deceitful/defective bow — one that bends in the wrong direction when drawn, sending the arrow off-target). The image captures the combination of outward form (they look like a bow, a weapon of God's army) and internal failure (they misfire when it counts).
58: wayyakʿîsûhû bĕbāmôtām — they made him angry with their hilltop shrines. Kāʿas (to provoke to anger, to irritate — often used of idolatry's effect on God). Bāmāh (high place — the unauthorized cult site on hills, the persistent target of prophetic and Deuteronomic criticism). ûbiphĕsîlêhem yaqnîʾûhû — and roused his jealousy with their carved idols. Qinʾāh (jealousy, zealous possessiveness — the emotion of a spouse discovering infidelity). The covenant is a marriage; idolatry is adultery.
59: shāmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm wayyitʿabbār — when God heard all this, he was incensed. ʿAbar (hithpael: to overflow with fury — as in v21 at the wilderness complaint). wayyimʾas meʾōd bĕyisrāʾêl — he thoroughly rejected Israel. Māʾas meʾōd (rejected intensely — the combination of the rejection verb with the intensifier meʾōd: very much, to a great degree) — not a mild disapproval but comprehensive rejection of what they had become.
60: wayyittōsh mishkān-shilōh — he abandoned the tabernacle at Shiloh. Nāṭash (to forsake, abandon — used of a field left fallow) — the dramatic abandonment of Shiloh, the central sanctuary before Jerusalem. Shilōh was the site of the tabernacle during the Judges period (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1:3) until the ark was captured in 1 Samuel 4. ʾōhel shikkēn bāʾādām — the tent he had established among his people. The tent God himself placed among his people — now abandoned.
61: wayyittēn lashshĕbî ʿuzzô — he allowed his strength to go into exile. ʿŌz (strength — a common epithet for the ark, cf. 1 Sam 4:21-22: kābôd departed from Israel when the ark was taken). wĕtiphʾartô bĕyad-ṣār — and his glory into enemy hands. Tiphʾāret (glory, splendor — parallel to ʿōz as paired terms for the ark). God himself handed his glory to the enemy — the ark capture is not a defeat of God but a divine concession of the symbol of his presence. The profound paradox: YHWH uses the enemy to judge his own people.
62: wayyasgēr laḥereb ʿammô — he surrendered his people to the sword. Sāgar (to shut up, surrender — the same verb as v48's surrender of Egyptian cattle to the hail). ûbĕnaḥălātô hitʾabbār — and turned against his inheritance in anger. ʾAbar (hithpael: overflow with fury) again — the same burning anger, now directed against Israel rather than Egypt. YHWH's inheritance (naḥălāh) has become the object of his own judgment.
63: baḥûrāyw ʾākĕlāh ʾesh — fire devoured their young men. The fire of war consuming the youth — the immediate cost of the Philistine battle (1 Sam 4:10-11). ûbĕtûlōtāyw lōʾ hullālû — and their young women received no marriage praise. Hālal (pual: to be praised, to have wedding songs sung over them) — the young women left unmarried because the young men were killed. The absence of wedding songs is a sign of social death; the generation that should have married and continued the people is extinguished.
64: kōhănāyw bĕḥereb nāphalû — their priests were cut down by the sword. Hophni and Phinehas (1 Sam 4:11) — the specific historical referent behind this verse. wĕʾalmĕnōtāyw lōʾ tibkênāh — their widows could not even weep. The wives of the slain priests could not complete the mourning rite — perhaps because they died too (Phinehas's wife died in childbirth upon hearing the news, 1 Sam 4:19-20), or because the scale of disaster overwhelmed the capacity to mourn. The absence of weeping is the deepest marker of catastrophe.
65: wayyîqaṣ kĕyāshēn ʾădōnāy — then the Lord awoke as if from sleep. The metaphor of God waking is startling — it implies that during the Shiloh period and ark-capture, God appeared to be sleeping (cf. Ps 44:23; Elijah's taunt of Baal in 1 Kgs 18:27). kĕgibbôr mithronān miyyāyin — like a warrior who rises vigorous from wine. The simile is deliberately bold: a warrior rousing from wine is re-energized, aggressive, formidable. God's action against the Philistines (1 Sam 5-7) is described in the register of warrior-arousal.
66: wayyak ṣārāyw ʾāḥôr — he drove his enemies back. The Philistines driven back — the plagues of 1 Sam 5 (tumors, mice) forcing them to return the ark. ḥerpat ʿôlām nātan lāmô — and put them to shame that will not end. Ḥerpat ʿôlām (everlasting reproach/shame) — the permanent disgrace of the nation that dared capture the ark of YHWH. The shame is not a moment but a status.
67: wayyimʾas bĕʾōhel yôsēph — he refused the tent of Joseph. The theological move of the psalm's climax: not merely punishing Israel but selecting among the tribes. Māʾas (to reject, refuse) applied to the tent of Joseph — the Shiloh tradition was associated with the Joseph tribes (Ephraim/Manasseh). ûbeshēbeṭ ʾeprāyim lōʾ bāḥār — and did not choose the tribe of Ephraim. The Ephraim failure introduced in v9 is now the theological basis for the tribal rejection. Ephraim's military failure was symptomatic of a deeper election-failure.
68: wayyibḥar ʾet-shēbeṭ yĕhûdāh — instead he chose the tribe of Judah. Bāḥar (to choose — the election verb of Deuteronomy, used of the divine choice of Israel, of Jerusalem, of the Levites, and now of Judah over Ephraim). ʾet-har ṣiyyôn ʾăsher ʾāhēb — Mount Zion, which he loved. ʾĀhēb (he loved — the divine love as the basis for election, not merit or size as in Deut 7:7-8). The choice of Zion is a love-choice.
69: wayyiben kĕmô-rāmîm miqdāshô — he built his sanctuary like the heights of heaven. Rāmîm (heights, the elevated places — the heavens) — Zion's temple built to cosmic proportions, like heaven itself. kĕʾereṣ yĕsādāh lĕʿôlām — like the earth that he has set in place forever. The sanctuary is as permanent as the earth's foundations — the comparison grounds Zion's permanence in creation-permanence. (The subsequent destruction of the temple in 587 BCE is why Ps 74 and 79 exist; this claim is covenant-eschatological, not empirical-political.)
70: wayyibḥar bĕdāwid ʿabdô — he chose David his servant. Bāḥar (the election verb) applied for the third time in four verses — Judah chosen (v68), Zion chosen (v68), David chosen (v70). The three choices are a single divine movement: tribe → city → king. wayyiqqāḥēhû mimmikmĕrôt ṣōʾn — and took him from the sheep pens. Mikhlāʾ ṣōʾn (sheep pens/enclosures) — the specific occupation of David before his anointing (1 Sam 16:11). The deliberate pastoral origin of the king.
71: mēʾăḥar ʿālôt hĕbîʾô — from tending the nursing ewes he brought him. ʿālôt (nursing ewes — the most demanding of the shepherd's flock, requiring special care for both ewe and lamb). David's competence with the most vulnerable sheep is the sign of his qualification for the most demanding of pastoral roles — shepherding the people. lirʿôt bĕyaʿăqōb ʿammô ûbĕyisrāʾêl naḥălātô — to shepherd Jacob his people, and Israel his inheritance. The transition from literal shepherd to metaphorical shepherd is explicit: the same verb (rāʿāh: to shepherd, tend) governs both the sheep and the nation.
72: wayrĕʿēm kĕtōm lĕbābô — he tended them with the integrity of his heart. Tōm lēbāb (completeness/integrity of heart — undivided, whole-hearted attention) — the internal quality of the shepherd's care. ûbitbûnat kappāyw yanḥēm — and guided them with the skill of his hands. Tĕbûnāh (understanding, skillfulness — the cognitive component of practical competence) applied to the hands — the intelligent, experienced work of the hands. Heart-integrity and hand-skill are the twin qualifications of the ideal shepherd-king, answering the indictment of the earlier generations who had neither.
